serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of stir-fried millet with chicken and bean sprouts is approximately 300 g, typically presented as a single plate at a Vietnamese restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central: Millet cooked (SR Legacy 20031), Chicken breast stir-fried (SR Legacy 05062), Mung bean sprouts raw (SR Legacy 11043)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct HPB Singapore or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry found for stir-fried millet with chicken and bean sprouts. Nutrient values estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data: cooked millet (~50% by weight), stir-fried chicken breast (~30% by weight), mung bean sprouts (~15% by weight), and cooking oil/seasonings (~5% by weight). Weighted composite calculated per 100 g of finished dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of millet-based foods and mixed grain dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked millet GI (GI=54, Atkinson et al. 2008) and bean sprout/chicken contribution
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for stir-fried millet with chicken and bean sprouts. GI estimated at 54 based on cooked millet GI of 54 (low GI, University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008). Chicken and bean sprouts contribute negligible glycemic load. Weighted GI of the dish approximated at 54 (low). Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (54 × 13.5 × 3) / 100 = 21.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and fish sauce
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g serving provides approximately 930 mg sodium, which represents a substantial portion of the 1500–2000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-sauce preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index from millet grain base
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 54 (low-moderate) and a glycemic load of ~21.9 per standard serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and pair this dish with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium load and moderate saturated fat from cooking oil and chicken
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish's sodium content (~930 mg per serving) and moderate saturated fat (~2.1 g per serving) are relevant for cardiovascular risk management; choosing lean chicken breast and minimal added oil reduces the saturated fat burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kê gà xào giá (Stir-Fried Millet with Chicken and Bean Sprouts) is a Vietnamese-origin dish served at Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore, notably at Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant. The dish features cooked millet (kê) stir-fried with sliced or shredded chicken (gà) and fresh mung bean sprouts (giá), seasoned with garlic, soy sauce, fish sauce, and a small amount of cooking oil. The texture is light and slightly chewy from the millet, with crisp bean sprouts providing contrast and tender chicken adding protein. The flavour profile is savoury and mildly umami, with a subtle nuttiness from the millet grain. Nutritionally, the dish provides a moderate energy density (~118 kcal per 100 g), a good protein content (~9.2 g per 100 g from chicken), and a relatively low fat content (~3.1 g per 100 g). The millet base contributes dietary fibre (~1.4 g per 100 g) and B vitamins including niacin and B6. The primary clinical concern is sodium from soy and fish sauce (~310 mg per 100 g), which is relevant for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The glycemic index is estimated at 54 (low-moderate), making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is dairy-free and considered halal when prepared with halal-certified chicken and sauces.

confidence_reason: No direct HPB Singapore, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or ICMR-NIN entry found for this specific dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for cooked millet, stir-fried chicken breast, and mung bean sprouts using a weighted composite approach. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked millet. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation methodology.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked millet (FDC ID 169702), chicken breast stir-fried (FDC ID 171477), and mung bean sprouts raw (FDC ID 169957). No single approved-source entry exists for this exact dish. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimate. 3. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for the component ingredients — no partially hydrogenated oils are used in traditional preparation. 4. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate based on cooked millet GI from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). 5. The Vietnamese name 'Kê gà xào giá' is preserved from the input record as the original-language dish name. 6. The dish is listed at SGD 20.00 at Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant on Circular Road, Singapore (GrabFood Singapore source). 7. FoodEx2 code A0CKP is used as closest_parent; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for millet-chicken-bean sprout stir-fry.
